summary: P010 is a highly organized, self-aware mature student who approaches food with strategic efficiency. At 30+, she's juggling full-time work, psychotherapy training, and clinical placements - a demanding schedule that shapes every food decision. Her meal prep mastery on Mondays sustains her through brutal 12-hour Wednesday/Thursday days, while weekend takeaways serve as earned rewards for surviving the week. She's articulate about her systems and motivations, using professional language that reflects her therapeutic training. Despite claiming to cook extensively, her actual behavior shows more variety - campus lunches, social eating out, and strategic delivery orders when energy is depleted. Her relationship with food is fundamentally functional: 'proper fuel' for a hectic life, though she genuinely enjoys the cooking process when time allows. She's financially comfortable enough to budget €425/month for food but remains value-conscious, particularly drawn to loyalty schemes and cashback systems that make spending feel strategic rather than indulgent.
personality_notes: Articulate, self-reflective, and systems-oriented. Uses professional therapeutic language and demonstrates high emotional intelligence. Organized but acknowledges her own contradictions with gentle humor.
food_relationship: Food is strategic fuel for an intense lifestyle, with cooking as both practical necessity and weekend creative outlet, punctuated by guilt-free delivery treats.
diary_summary: Actual eating pattern shows balanced mix of meal-prepped home cooking (57% of meals), strategic convenience choices (campus food, eating out), and one planned weekend delivery order - more varied than interview suggested.
say_do_gaps: Claims to cook 'a lot' but diary shows only 57% home-cooked meals with regular use of campus food and eating out
Emphasizes Saturday night delivery routine but diary shows Friday delivery order when alone and depleted
Describes rigid meal prep schedule but diary shows flexibility with baked oats when working from home

key_moments.description: Explains cooking as 'proper fuel' for hectic weeks, contrasting with feeling 'like crap' after eating out
key_moments.significance: Reveals core food philosophy - nutrition as performance optimization rather than pleasure

key_moments.description: Describes grocery shopping as 'one of my hate most hated chores' and calls it a 'first world problem'
key_moments.significance: Shows self-awareness about privilege while revealing genuine stress around food procurement

key_moments.description: Would still choose Deliveroo over Just Eat even if €2 more expensive because of tracking feature
key_moments.significance: Demonstrates how control and predictability matter more than price for this organized personality

what_not_said: Any mention of food enjoyment or pleasure beyond 'looking forward to dinners'
Social eating with housemates despite living together
Any cooking failures or food waste from meal prep system
Financial stress despite being a full-time working student
initial_hypotheses: Her meal prep system is likely more flexible than described - diary shows adaptation based on work location and social opportunities
Delivery serves as both reward and emergency backup when systems fail (Friday night with empty fridge)
Her mature student status creates unique value expectations - wants student pricing but has adult income and standards
The therapeutic training influences how she rationalizes and explains her food choices with professional language
questions_for_coding: How do other mature students balance efficiency with food enjoyment?
What role does meal prep play in managing study/work stress for this demographic?
How does having adult income but student status affect delivery app expectations?
Do other participants use professional language to distance themselves from 'student' behaviors?
